fields.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)
fields.definition: A neurological and developmental disorder that affects how people interact with others, communicate, learn, and behave. Symptoms generally appear in the first two years of life and can be diagnosed at any age. ASD is characterized by differences in social communication and interaction, and by restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior or interests. The disorder varies widely in severity and presentation across individuals.
fields.example: One person with ASD may be nonverbal and need significant daily support; another may be highly verbal and independent but struggle with social nuance and sensory input.

fields.name: Social Communication Differences (ASD)
fields.definition: One of the two core diagnostic features of ASD. Includes persistent differences in social-emotional reciprocity (e.g., difficulty with back-and-forth conversation, reduced sharing of interests or emotions), nonverbal communication (e.g., limited eye contact, difficulty reading facial expressions or body language), and developing and maintaining relationships appropriate to developmental level. These differences are present across contexts and are not explained by intellectual disability alone.
fields.example: Struggling to recognize when a conversation partner wants to end a discussion, or missing sarcasm and speaking very literally.

fields.name: Restricted and Repetitive Behaviors (ASD)
fields.definition: The second core diagnostic feature of ASD. Includes at least two of: stereotyped or repetitive motor movements or speech (e.g., lining up objects, echolalia); insistence on sameness and inflexible adherence to routines; highly fixated interests that are abnormal in intensity or focus; and hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input. These behaviors must cause impairment and be present across multiple contexts.
fields.example: Insisting on the exact same route to school every day, extensive focus on a single niche topic, or spinning objects repeatedly.

fields.name: Sensory Sensitivities in ASD
fields.definition: Many autistic people experience hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input — sounds, textures, lights, smells, pain, or temperature. Hypersensitivity means the nervous system amplifies sensory information, causing what might seem minor to others (a tag in a shirt, background noise) to be overwhelmingly uncomfortable or painful. Hyposensitivity means reduced response to sensory input, which can lead to sensory-seeking behavior such as rocking or touching textures. Sensory differences are now formally recognized in DSM-5 ASD criteria.
fields.example: A person with ASD cannot tolerate fluorescent lighting or the texture of certain foods, significantly limiting environments and diet.

fields.name: The Spectrum Concept in ASD
fields.definition: The term 'spectrum' in Autism Spectrum Disorder reflects the wide range of symptoms, skills, and levels of functioning across individuals with ASD. No two autistic people are identical — some are highly verbal with average or above-average intelligence; others are minimally verbal or have intellectual disabilities. ASD encompasses what were previously separate diagnoses (Autistic Disorder, Asperger's Syndrome, PDD-NOS) under one umbrella since DSM-5 in 2013. Support needs vary from minimal accommodations to intensive daily assistance.
fields.example: Two people both diagnosed with ASD may have completely different presentations — one working as a software engineer with strong language skills, another requiring full-time care with limited speech.

fields.body: There is no biological test — no blood test or brain scan — for ASD. Diagnosis relies on behavioral evaluation. A clinician observes and assesses the person's behavior and development, often using standardized tools like the Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule (ADOS). Information is gathered from caregivers or family members about developmental history. Screening typically begins in early childhood: pediatricians screen for developmental delays at 9, 18, and 24 or 30 months, with ASD-specific screening at 18 and 24 months. ASD can usually be reliably diagnosed by age 2, though many individuals — especially those without intellectual disability or with good language skills — are not diagnosed until adolescence or adulthood.
fields.name: How ASD Is Diagnosed
fields.example: A 3-year-old is flagged at a well-child visit for limited eye contact and not responding to their name. A multidisciplinary team conducts a full developmental evaluation and confirms ASD.

fields.title: Early Signs of ASD in Young Children
fields.body: Early signs of ASD can appear in the first year of life and typically become clearer by 18–24 months. Signs to watch for include: not responding to one's name by 12 months; not pointing to objects of interest by 14 months; not playing 'pretend' by 18 months; avoiding eye contact or preferring to be alone; repeating words or phrases (echolalia); becoming very upset by minor changes in routine; having very focused or intense interests; flapping hands, rocking, or spinning; unusual reactions to sounds, smells, tastes, or textures; and losing language or social skills after achieving them (regression). Early identification and intervention are associated with better long-term outcomes.

fields.question: How common is ASD?
fields.answer: According to CDC data cited by NIMH, approximately 1 in 36 children (about 2.8%) were identified with ASD in the 2023 report, up from 1 in 44 (2.3%) in the previous estimate. ASD is 3.4 times as prevalent among boys (4.9%) as among girls (1.4%). ASD occurs in all racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic groups.

fields.question: What proportion of autistic people also have an intellectual disability?
fields.answer: Among children identified with ASD, approximately 37.9% are classified as also having an intellectual disability (IQ below 70). The remaining majority have average or above-average intelligence. This means that intellectual disability is common but not universal in ASD, and the two diagnoses are separate and can occur independently.

fields.name: Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA)
fields.definition: A behavioral therapy approach widely used for ASD, particularly in children. ABA uses principles of learning theory to reinforce desired behaviors and reduce harmful or interfering ones. It is delivered in intensive sessions and is often individualized to target communication, social skills, self-care, and academic skills. Early and intensive ABA (20–40 hours per week) has the strongest research support for improving outcomes in ASD. Modern ABA emphasizes naturalistic, child-led approaches over older, more rigid drill-based formats.
fields.example: A therapist uses positive reinforcement to help a nonverbal child with ASD communicate wants by pointing to pictures, expanding to simple words over time.

fields.name: Speech-Language Therapy for ASD
fields.definition: A treatment addressing communication differences in ASD. Speech-language pathologists (SLPs) work on verbal communication (articulation, language development, conversation skills), augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) for nonverbal individuals, and pragmatic language — the social use of language such as understanding context, turn-taking, and interpreting nonliteral speech like idioms and sarcasm. Early speech-language intervention is associated with improved communication outcomes across the spectrum.
fields.example: An SLP introduces a communication tablet (AAC device) to a minimally verbal autistic child, enabling them to express needs and participate in classroom activities.

fields.title: ASD and Co-Occurring Conditions
fields.body: Autistic people frequently have one or more co-occurring conditions that require separate diagnosis and treatment. Common co-occurring conditions include: ADHD (very common; inattention and hyperactivity are frequently present alongside ASD features); anxiety disorders (especially social anxiety and generalized anxiety); depression (more common in autistic adolescents and adults); epilepsy/seizure disorders (present in roughly 20–30% of autistic people); gastrointestinal problems (pain, constipation, food selectivity); sleep disorders; and intellectual disability (in approximately 38% of cases). Identifying and treating these co-occurring conditions is an important part of comprehensive ASD care.

fields.question: Is there a gender difference in ASD diagnosis rates?
fields.answer: Yes, a substantial one. ASD is diagnosed 3.4 times as often in boys (prevalence 4.9%) as in girls (1.4%). Research suggests this reflects both a genuine difference in prevalence and a significant diagnostic gap: girls and women with ASD are more likely to 'mask' or camouflage autistic traits through learned social imitation, making their symptoms less visible and leading to later or missed diagnoses.

fields.name: Neurodiversity Perspective
fields.definition: A perspective — prominent in autistic self-advocacy communities — that frames neurological differences such as autism as natural variation in human brain development rather than deficits requiring a cure. From this perspective, support should focus on reducing barriers and providing accommodations rather than changing autistic traits. Many autistic self-advocates prefer identity-first language ('autistic person') over person-first language ('person with autism'), though individual preferences vary. NIMH's clinical focus is on interventions that improve functioning and reduce distress — which can include accommodation-based approaches depending on individual needs and goals.
fields.example: An autistic adult advocates for workplace accommodations (noise-canceling headphones, written instructions) rather than trying to change their neurological processing style.

fields.question: Do vaccines cause autism?
fields.answer: No. The scientific consensus — supported by major health agencies — is that vaccines do not cause autism. The claim originated from a 1998 paper that was subsequently retracted due to data fraud and ethical violations. Research consistently shows ASD involves genetic and neurological factors present from early development. NIMH research into ASD causes focuses on genes, brain development, and prenatal factors — not vaccination. Delaying or avoiding vaccination based on this debunked claim creates real public health risk with no basis in evidence.
